sentences.text: My name is Moshe-- M-O-S-H-E-- Rojek-- R-O-J-E-K.
I was born in 13 of July in 1933.
In Warsaw, Poland.
Since I was six years old, so there are not that many things that I remember from before the war till 1939.
But there are a few things that I-- that I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I remember, for instance, my father wasn't religious, even so his father was.
But he didn't know his father, since his father died when he was about four years old.
And by the way, he grew up in Janusz Korczak's orphanage.
So over there, he didn't get really a religious upraising.
So he wasn't religious.
And in his ideology, he was leftist.
And-- and he-- in our house, religion wasn't really a factor, except that we lived with our grandmother, which-- What was her name?
Her name was Chaya.
It was my father's mother, and she was very religious.

And she tried to introduce some religion to me, which, in later years, had quite a significance [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because she tried to send me-- on our block where we lived, there was a synagogue with a heder, which is a place where you send children in very young age, about 5 or so, to study Hebrew and prayers and so on.
So she secretly, without my father's knowledge, used to send me there to study Hebrew reading and prayers.
And also, when she would feed me, she would make sure that I put a kippah and make the prayers before the eating.
That's a story.
I must have been somewhere around four-- between four and five years old-- around five years old maybe, which I guess that's one of the earliest memories of mine.
And one day, my father happened to walk in while I was eating with the kippah, and he made a big fuss of it, and he was very mad at my-- at my grandmother.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is one episode that I remember.
There was another episode.
And that must have been maybe about a year or more even later than that and must have been somewhere in the summer of '39, where I was going to a summer day camp.
And my father used to bring me to that place where they used to take the children from there out of town.
And since I-- and my mother used to prepare some lunch for me to take for the day.
And I was a very bad eater.
So I used to take the lunch, and at the corner where we were standing there was a fence-- a wooden fence.
And that fence had a hole in-- in it, and while I was standing and waiting for the-- for the bus or whatever it was to come and pick us up, I used to threw in that lunch behind that fence.
One day, I wasn't so successful, and my father-- my father caught me.
So that was another episode that I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was another episode that must have been also probably before the war already, shortly before the war, and that was on [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Passover time.
And my father took me to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a public-- what's it called-- baths.
There was a bath, steamer bath, because the facilities weren't that modern with showers and [INAUDIBLE] in the houses.
So we-- my father took me with him, and it was, as I mentioned, Passover.
And after the baths, he bought me a hot dog and a bun.
But he warned me not to tell my grandmother because it was Passover.
So that was another episode that I remember from my-- Did you tell your grandmother?
No, I did not.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, I remember also that I went to a kindergarten.
I didn't go to school yet, because in Poland you started school at age of seven.
Since I wasn't seven yet, so I went to kindergarten.
And-- and my aunt, my father's older sister, she was the teacher there.
It was a school and a kindergarten and-- that belonged to the Jewish community of Warsaw, and I attended there at that school, at that kindergarten.
I remembered a lot.
And as a matter of fact, two years ago, we were there, and I was there with my cousin, my aunt's daughter.
And I told her-- I told her everything-- how the school looked.
And where the yard was, where we were playing, there was a synagogue on the yard, on those facilities.
And I told her I remembered the man that was the caretaker of the grounds and of the synagogue, and I described it to her.
I even remembered where-- they were living in the same building, and I showed her where.
And-- and she even asked me-- now, they were living in two places.
One of the places was on the-- on the first floor, and the other one was on the-- on the third floor, because it was a three or four-story building.
And she told me I know where we lived-- because she is younger.
She is three years younger than me.
And she told me, I know-- I know where we lived on the first floor, because that was the last place where they were living when the-- when the war-- when they escaped from Warsaw.
But I don't know where we lived on the last-- on the last floor [INAUDIBLE].
So I showed her where.
I remembered everything.
As a matter of fact--
Well, not much.
I remember-- I remember how we used to play, how we used to -- the room where we played, how we used to go out on the recess, where we used to go out on the recess.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember the people.
I don't remember the faces anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I remember the facility in general-- the building, the courtyard, the surroundings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That's about all I can remember of that.
What were your parents like?
My parents, they were both-- their fathers on my father's side and of my mother's side, were both-- they died when my parents were very young.
As a result of it, their economic situation was bad.
They weren't rich people.
My father, when his father died, he was about four.
He was the youngest one.
And they were four children.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother was, I guess, also around that age when her father died.
And they-- they were only two.
My uncle-- she had a brother, which was my uncle-- was a few years older than her, and my mother.
And very bad conditions, like, apparently when my grandfather lived, they were doing very well.
But after--
About whose personalities?
Oh, my parents.
Well, I was six years old when I lost my father.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] He was only 29 years old.
[DRINKS]
So I can't remember much.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, like, the few episodes that I said that were about him.
I actually remember one other thing about him.
And it was probably also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in '39, where, in the summer, he took my brother and me out somewhere [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a beach, which I remember till today.
My brother's name is Arieh.
His name is Raveh today.
He Hebrew-tized his name.
He changed his name to Hebrew, and he's Raveh.
He lives in Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He has a family-- three children, a daughter and two sons.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] He is almost two years younger than me.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] About maybe going back to what I remember about the personalities, as I say, I know from-- from what I-- from stories, that, and as I mentioned before, my father grew up in Janusz Korczak's orphanage, and he got a very well education there.
Well, that's what that I had said that I remembered, that he took us to the beach in that summer of probably 1939.
That probably was the last summer before the war.
He took us there.
We had a very good time and we enjoyed, and I remember it.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My father, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he-- he was-- he was well-educated in Janusz Korczak, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but he didn't have-- he wanted to continue studies.
But because they were poor, he couldn't continue.
But from friends that-- his colleagues that I met years after-- everybody used to tell me the same thing, that how smart and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] and bright my father was.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and that's maybe-- maybe one of the reasons why he got killed.
It was also because of that, because he was involved in politics.
And he was afraid when the Germans were to come in, they'll-- he'll be on the list, and they will execute him.
So.
My father had three brothers, and they all worked, since they were all poor.
And then they had a relative-- I think it was an uncle of theirs-- that had a bakery, a big bakery in Warsaw.
So when they came of age-- so they one after the other went into that bakery.
And they worked there, and that's how they make the living.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wasn't educated, again, because of the conditions that she had.
And after they-- they married at a young age, and my mother went to work as well, and my father was working, and--
In a factory.
I don't know what kind of factory, and I don't even know if it was the same factory where she worked [INAUDIBLE].
She kept on changing positions.
I'm not sure about that.
I don't know really that.
But I know that she was working all her years since young-- since she was very young, and she was helping and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and supporting the family.
We weren't religious, I said.
And-- but I don't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't recall really [INAUDIBLE]-- all this ended anyways when I was only six.
I don't recall any lack of-- I'll say we were well fed and dressed, and that's it, you know.
And we lived in a small apartment, nothing great.
I saw the apartment just-- I remembered everything about that apartment.
I still remember.
And two years ago when we were in Warsaw, I went to visit.
And I didn't have the chance to go in and see it on the inside, but I saw the surroundings.
It was a small apartment-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two rooms, one big room that was the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the bedroom and the living room and the dining room.
And there was another big room, which was the kitchen.
And my grandmother used to-- there was a bed there too, and the table and chairs.
So my grandmother used to live there, and we used to eat there too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But since I was only six, I couldn't know that if we are rich or poor.
It was enough for me, and I didn't feel any shortage of-- any lack of anything.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then the war broke out, 1939.
And my father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] decided before the Germans come in, he'll run away from Warsaw, and he'll go to Russia.
And once he established himself, he finds a place where to stay, he'll bring us over.
However, it didn't work out according to the plans.
And on the way there, he was shot by the Germans.
And since we didn't hear-- and then the Germans came into Warsaw.
I remember-- I remember the time before they came in.
There was-- they bombed Warsaw very heavily.

We decided at that time, during the bombardment of Warsaw-- like we lived on the other side of the city of Warsaw, like, which-- which was called Praga, which is kind of the other side of the Vistula, which divided Warsaw.
So we lived there, and mother's-- my mother's mother lived in Warsaw.
Her name was Leah.
So apparently they decided to go and be with my grandmother there.
So we went there to Warsaw.
And then the bombardment of Warsaw started, and it was a very hard time.
The bombardment was very heavy.
And from what I was told later, I remembered that too, that they apparently bombed Warsaw with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] burning bombs.
So the whole city was burning.
And as soon as we went into one building for shelter, it was bombed.
So we ran out from one building, ran to the other building.
And by the time we came to the other building, the other building was burning.
So we were running like a mouse from one hole to the other, and the whole city was running.
And you saw dead people and children all over the streets.
And I remember a little child sitting and crying, mommy, mommy, and the mother was dead [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the street.
And dead were-- dead people were all over.
And destruction was all over [INAUDIBLE].
So we stayed.
But finally, the-- the-- the bombardment ended, and then we decided to go back to our apartment.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we were waiting for letters from-- from-- from our father, and they didn't come.
We didn't know that he was already dead by that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since my mother-- we didn't have any money, and my father was gone.
So my mother decided that she'll would take me, six years old, and my-- my younger brother, four years old, and we know where he was supposed to be, and we would go look for him there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, my grandmother-- my mother's mother-- was very much against it.
My mother, at that time, was maybe only about 24 years old.
So she didn't trust her to take two little children and go somewhere in a foreign country.
So she-- she didn't agree to it, and there was a big argument about that.
And my grandmother says, I'm not going to say good-bye to you.
I'm not going to talk to you.
But my mother decided that she'll go anyways, because she says, I have nothing to lose here.
I can't support two children.
And if I find [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father, at least we'll be together and whatever  will be.
So we-- we left.
We left without money and with very little possession.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By that time, we were six weeks in Warsaw with the Germans, and things progressively got worse and worse and worse.
Killing already started.
Shooting started.
I remember across our house, they were running after some-- they wanted to take somebody to work, a Jewish fellow.
He escaped, and they were running after him and shooting right in front of our window.
And progressively, things were getting worse.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So my mother said, that's it.
And she took us both, and we started to go.
We reached the Russian border.
By that time, it was already sealed off.
For a time, between the [INAUDIBLE], that they were open and people used to go from one place to the other.
By that time, it was already sealed off, and we couldn't really pass.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were sitting there for about two weeks, right on the open grounds.
And thousands and thousands of people were sitting there and waiting for a chance.
Maybe they'll open their borders.
Maybe we could pass and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] waiting for a chance to pass to  the other side.
I'll tell you truthfully, it was very bad.
I know it wasn't much what to eat, because it was a closed field, a huge field, and there was nothing around it.
So I don't know what really we used to-- I think there were some stuff that my grandmother that lived with us cooked and prepared [INAUDIBLE] and some bread and what that we had with us.
And I guess that's what we ate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is one episode, actually, that I remember.
I always remember.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [DRINKS] We were coming close to the border, and there was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was a fence there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And beyond the fence, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you couldn't pass, only if you passed a certain gate.
At that gate was standing Gestapo, SS men, and a Pole.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Germans actually couldn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tell a difference between a Jew and a Pole, unless he was bearded with, you know, a typical Jewish person, like all traditional.
But other than that, they couldn't really tell a difference.
The ones that guided them were the Poles.
I was six years old, and I still remember it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to the gate.
There was that Gestapo and the Pole standing there.
And the Pole would point out-- Pole, Jew, Pole, Jew.
And the Jews, they were sent aside, couldn't pass the gate, and they let the Poles pass.
Somehow, I guess, we found an opening somewhere.
We went along the fence there for quite some while, and we looked and looked and looked.
And somehow we find an opening somewhere, and we just got ourselves through.
Yeah.
